segments.paradiso-2-g50: This segment is punctuation only: a full stop marking the end of a preceding sentence. There are no lexical items to analyse.
segments.paradiso-2-g51: Ella = subject pronoun 'she'. sorrise is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd singular of sorridere, used here in narrative/poetic register to mean 'smiled'. alquanto is an adverb meaning 'somewhat' or 'a little' modifying the verb.
segments.paradiso-2-g52: e poi = 'and then', a connective that introduces what follows. S’elli is an archaic contraction (S’ = se 'if', + elli) introducing a conditional; here it functions as 'if' (archaic/poetic). erra is present (3rd sg) of errare 'to err' and l’oppinïon is the (inverted) subject 'the opinion' — the clause means 'if the opinion errs'.
segments.paradiso-2-g53: mi = dative/clitic 'to me'; disse is passato remoto (3rd sg) of dire 'said'. This is the speech tag linking the quotation to the speaker.
segments.paradiso-2-g54: d’i mortali = 'of the mortals' / 'among mortals' (di + the plural article i). dove introduces a relative/locative clause 'where'. chiave di senso is a metaphorical 'key of sense/understanding'; non diserra = 'does not unbolt/open' (diserrare = to unfasten/unlock). The whole phrase: 'concerning mortals, where the key of understanding does not unlock.'
segments.paradiso-2-g55: certo functions as an adverb 'certainly' (here strengthening the assertion). non ... punger: with the modal dovrien (contracted conditional of dovere) plus infinitive punger (pungere 'to prick/sting'), the structure means 'the shafts ought not to sting you' — li strali (the arrows/shafts) is the logical subject placed after the verb (inverted order). d’ammirazione = 'of/with astonishment' and omai = 'anymore/by now/nowadays' (temporal adverb).
segments.paradiso-2-g56: poi dietro ai sensi = 'for, after (or behind) the senses' (dietro + article ai = a + i). vedi is 2nd singular present of vedere, here meaning 'you see' / 'you understand'. che introduces the clause 'that reason has short wings' — la ragione (reason) ha (has) corte l’ali (literally 'short the wings', here 'short wings'), a metaphor saying reason's reach is limited compared with the senses.
segments.paradiso-2-g57: Ma = 'but' introducing a contrast. dimmi is the imperative 2nd sg of dire with the enclitic mi 'tell me' (imperative + object clitic). quel che introduces the content-question clause 'what'. tu da te = 'you from yourself' = emphatic 'you yourself' (da te = 'of/by yourself'). ne is a pronominal particle 'of it/about it' placed before the verb; pensi is present 2nd sg of pensare 'you think'.
segments.paradiso-2-g58: E io = 'And I' — the speaker (I) introduces his reply. E is the conjunction 'and'; io is the first-person pronoun 'I'.
segments.paradiso-2-g59: Ciò che = 'that which' (a relative nominal clause). n’ is an enclitic (here functioning as a reduplicated particle: in Dante 'n’appar' stands for 'ne appare' and indicates the relationship of the appearance to the speaker/context — roughly 'appears to/for us'). appar is the verb 'appear/seem' (present), qua sù = 'here above' (qua = here, sù = up). diverso = 'different'. credo is the main verb 'I believe' introducing the content clause che fanno i corpi rari e densi; fanno (3rd pl of fare) here means 'make' or 'cause' (i corpi rari e densi = 'rare and dense bodies' are the agent/subject causing the appearance). The clause thus asserts that the perceived difference is produced by bodies that are rare and dense.
segments.paradiso-2-g36: S’ is the archaic contraction of se (if) introducing a conditional; io is the subject 'I'; era is the imperfect of essere used here in a conditional/reflective sense ('were/was'); corpo is the predicate noun. The clause literally reads 'If I were a body,' with the imperfect used where modern Italian would prefer a subjunctive.
segments.paradiso-2-g37: e 'and'; qui 'here/at this place or point in the argument'; non si concepe is an impersonal present (si + verb) meaning 'it is not conceivable' or 'one cannot conceive'; com' = come 'how/as'; una dimensione altra = 'another/other dimension/measure' (adjective after noun, poetic order); patio is an archaic/poetic form (contextually a verbal idea 'to suffer/endure') and is obscure in modern usage.
segments.paradiso-2-g38: ch' = che 'that' introducing a subordinate clause; esser convien is an impersonal construction (convien + infinitive) meaning 'it is necessary/it must be that' (here 'that it be'); se is the conditional 'if' (though the provided senses list is not an exact match); corpo in corpo is a prepositional phrase 'body into/within body'; repe is an archaic/obscure form (context implies something like 'returns' or 'repeats').
segments.paradiso-2-g39: accender (infinitive) 'to kindle/arouse'; ne is a clitic meaning 'of it/about it' or 'in me' depending on scope (here 'in me' or 'of that'); dovria is the conditional/modal 'should/would have to' (dovrebbe in modern Italian); più 'any longer/no more'; il disio 'the desire' is the grammatical subject of the following infinitive phrase; di veder = 'to see' (infinitive governed by disio); quella essenza 'that essence'; in che si vede = 'in which one sees' (si = reflexive/impersonal, vede = is seen/one sees); come nostra natura e Dio s’unio = 'how our nature and God s’unio' — s’unio is an archaic/poetic perfect/past form meaning 'were united' (si + unìo), so the clause says in which is seen how our nature and God were united.
segments.paradiso-2-g40: Lì 'there' (locative/adverb); si vedrà is the impersonal/passive future ('it will be seen' / 'one will see'); ciò che 'that which/what' introduces a relative clause; tenem is an archaic first-person-plural present 'teniamo' meaning 'we hold/keep/regard'; per fede 'by faith' (per = by/through, fede = faith).
segments.paradiso-2-g41: non is the negation 'not'; dimostrato is the past participle of dimostrare used adjectivally = 'demonstrated/proved' (here negated: 'not demonstrated'). The comma signals an appositive parenthesis.
segments.paradiso-2-g42: ma 'but'; fia is an archaic/future form meaning 'will be' (future of essere); per sé 'in itself' or 'for itself' (per = by/for, sé = itself); noto = 'known' (adjectival); a guisa = 'in the manner/likeness'; del ver primo = 'of the first truth' (del = of the, ver = true/truth, primo = first); che l’uom crede = 'which man believes' (relative clause; l’ = the, uom = uomo 'man', crede = present 'believes').
segments.paradiso-2-g43: Io 'I' (subject); rispuosi is the passato remoto first-person singular of rispondere — 'I answered'. The colon marks direct speech that follows.
segments.paradiso-2-g44: Madonna is a vocative 'My lady' (addressing Beatrice/the Virgin in different contexts); left as a direct form of address.
segments.paradiso-2-g45: sì devoto = 'so/that devout' or 'as devout' (sì = so/thus, devoto = devoted/devout); com' = come 'as' introducing a comparative clause; esser posso = inverted periphrasis where the infinitive 'esser' precedes the modal 'posso' (modern Italian would have 'posso essere'); più modifies the ability ('as devout as I can be').
segments.paradiso-2-g46: ringrazio 'I thank' (present tense used to state gratitude now); lui 'him' (direct object); lo qual = 'who/which' (lo + qual forming a relative 'he who'); dal mortal mondo = 'from the mortal world' (dal = da + il); m’ ha remoto = 'has removed me' — m’ is the clitic 'mi' (me), ha is auxiliary avere, remoto is past participle/adjective 'removed/distant'.
segments.paradiso-2-g47: Ma 'but'; ditemi is the imperative (second-person plural) or present-subjunctive used as a polite imperative: 'tell me'. The colon introduces the question that follows.
segments.paradiso-2-g48: che son = 'what are' or 'which are' (che introducing an interrogative/relative); li segni bui = 'the dark signs/marks' (li = article, segni = marks, bui = dark/obscure); di questo corpo = 'of this body' (di = of, questo = this, corpo = body). The phrase asks for the identity or nature of the marks on the body.
segments.paradiso-2-g49: che = 'which/that' introducing a relative clause; là giuso in terra = 'there below on earth' (là = there, giuso = down/below — an archaic/poetic spelling of 'giù'; in terra = on earth/down on the earth); fan = fanno 'they make/cause' (present); di Cain favoleggiare altrui is a causative construction: 'make others tell fables/stories about Cain' (di Cain = about Cain, favoleggiare = to tell fables, altrui = others). Word order is poetic: object and indirect object elements are arranged for rhythm.
segments.paradiso-2-g1: A direct address: 'O voi' is a vocative 'O you'. The relative clause 'che siete in piccioletta barca' uses the present indicative 'siete' (2nd person plural) to state their condition; 'in piccioletta barca' is a prepositional phrase where the feminine adjective 'piccioletta' agrees with 'barca'.
segments.paradiso-2-g2: 'desiderosi' is an adjective agreeing with the addressed 'voi' meaning 'eager'; it takes 'di' + infinitive, here contracted to 'd’'. 'ascoltar' is the infinitive with the final -e dropped (common in poetry) and means 'to listen'.
segments.paradiso-2-g3: 'seguiti' is a past participle used adjectivally (understood as 'having followed') modifying the addressees. 'dietro al mio legno' = 'behind my ship/wood' (legno used figuratively for a boat). The relative clause 'che cantando varca' has 'che' = 'which', 'cantando' a present participle 'singing', and 'varca' present indicative (3rd sg) of 'varcare' meaning 'plows/crosses (the sea)'.
segments.paradiso-2-g4: 'tornate' is here a 2nd person plural imperative (command) 'turn back' (it can look like present indicative but the context is an exhortation). 'a riveder' = 'to see again' with the infinitive shortened in poetry; 'li vostri liti' = 'your shores' uses the archaic plural article 'li'.
segments.paradiso-2-g5: 'non vi mettete' is the negative 2nd pl. imperative 'do not place/put yourselves' (vi is the reflexive object meaning 'yourselves'). 'in pelago' is a prepositional phrase 'into the open sea' (pelago = the high/open sea).
segments.paradiso-2-g6: 'ché' is a conjunction here meaning 'for' or 'because' introducing the reason; 'forse' is an adverb 'perhaps' giving a tentative cause.
segments.paradiso-2-g7: 'perdendo' is the gerund of 'perdere' ('losing') expressing a circumstance or conditional sense ('if you lose'); 'me' is the direct object pronoun 'me/myself'. Together they mean 'if (by) losing me'.
segments.paradiso-2-g8: 'rimarreste' is the 2nd person plural conditional ('you would remain/you would be left'), and 'smarriti' is a past participle used adjectivally 'lost/bewildered', agreeing in number and gender. The phrase says what would happen if they lost the speaker.
segments.paradiso-2-g9: 'L’acqua' is the subject; 'ch’io prendo' is a relative clause 'that I take/draw' (prendo in present). The archaic negative phrase 'già mai non si corse' means 'has never been run/plied before' — 'si corse' is an impersonal or reflexive past meaning 'was run/flowed', and the triple adverbial negatives ('già mai non') emphasize 'never' in older usage.
segments.paradiso-2-g10: Minerva is the subject (the goddess/personification of wisdom); 'spira' is 3rd singular present of 'spirare' meaning literally 'breathes' but used figuratively as 'inspires'.
segments.paradiso-2-g11: 'e' = 'and'; 'conducemi' is 'conduce' (3rd sg) with the enclitic pronoun 'mi' attached, meaning 'leads me' or 'guides me'; 'Appollo' is the god Apollo (here the poetic patron of poetry).
segments.paradiso-2-g12: 'e nove Muse' = 'and nine Muses' (plural subject); 'mi dimostran' is 3rd pl. present 'show to me' (dimostran = dimostrano) with enclitic 'mi' as indirect object; 'l’Orse' (the Bears) is the thing shown — likely constellations used as a poetic/navigation image.
segments.paradiso-2-g13: 'Voialtri pochi' addresses a small group ('you few'). The relative clause 'che drizzaste il collo per tempo al pan de li angeli' uses 'drizzaste' (passato remoto, 2nd pl.) 'you raised/straightened your neck' metaphorically 'you looked up early' toward 'al pan de li angeli' ('the bread of the angels' = divine bread). 'per tempo' = 'in time/early'.
segments.paradiso-2-g14: 'del quale' (of which) refers back to 'pan' (the bread). 'vivesi qui' is impersonal present 'one lives here' or 'people live here'; 'ma non sen vien satollo' means literally 'but one does not come away sated' — 'sen vien' is an archaic combination (se + ne + vien) meaning 'comes away from it'.
segments.paradiso-2-g15: The verbal phrase shows modal inversion typical of poetry: the infinitive 'metter' (to put/place) precedes 'potete' (you can), giving 'you can put' or 'you may set'. 'ben' is emphatic 'well'. 'per l’alto sale' = 'on the high/open sea' (poetic image), and 'vostro navigio' = 'your vessel'.
segments.paradiso-2-g16: 'servando' is a gerund/participle of 'servare' here meaning 'keeping' or 'observing'; 'mio solco' = 'my furrow/wake' (solco used metaphorically for the course left by the speaker). 'dinanzi a l’acqua' = 'ahead of the water'; the relative clause 'che ritorna equale' ('that returns equal') describes the water that 'comes back unchanged', so they should keep the speaker's track before that returning water.
segments.paradiso-2-g17: 'Que’ glorïosi' = 'Those glorious ones' (Que' = quei). 'che passaro al Colco' uses the passato remoto 'passaro' (they passed/went) to Colchis; 'non s’ammiraron' is passato remoto with reflexive 'si' meaning 'they were not amazed'; 'come voi farete' looks forward to the future: 'as you will (be amazed)'.
segments.paradiso-2-g18: 'quando' = 'when'; 'Iasón' is the subject Jason; 'vider' is an archaic past-tense form (passato remoto) of 'vedere' meaning 'saw'; the construction 'vider fatto bifolco' means 'saw himself made / found himself made a bifolco' where 'fatto' is a past participle 'made/turned into' and 'bifolco' is a peasant/boor (the humiliating transformation).
segments.paradiso-2-g19: This whole clause names the force doing the carrying (La concreata e perpetüa sete del deïforme regno) and then gives the verb and adverbial phrase. 'La concreata e perpetüa sete' is a noun phrase with two coordinated adjectives ('concreata' = hardened/consolidated; 'perpetüa' = perpetual) modifying 'sete' (here 'thirst'); 'del deïforme regno' is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of the misshapen kingdom'. 'cen' is an archaic/clitic contraction (ce + n') meaning roughly 'from there / from it to us' and attaches to the verb; 'portava' is imperfect indicative 'bore/carried' (3rd singular) describing the action; the adverb 'veloci' (plural form used adverbially) and the comparative clause 'quasi come ’l ciel vedete' ('almost/as you see the heavens') qualify the speed. Word order is natural: subject noun-phrase → clitic adverbial → verb → manner/comparison.
segments.paradiso-2-g20: 'Beatrice in suso' places the proper name first and the locative phrase after it: 'in suso' is an archaic expression meaning 'up above' (like Modern Italian 'in suso'/'sopra'). The short phrase simply locates Beatrice high above; word order is subject + locative.
segments.paradiso-2-g21: The conjunction 'e' links clauses. 'io' is the emphatic subject pronoun (normally unnecessary in Italian but used here for emphasis), 'in lei' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'upon/within her' (i.e. looking at her), and 'guardava' is the imperfect indicative (1st person singular) meaning 'I was gazing' or 'I looked.' The imperfect gives a continuing or habitual sense to the action.
segments.paradiso-2-g22: 'e forse in tanto in quanto' is a temporal/comparative formula, roughly 'and perhaps in as much time/as briefly as.' 'forse' = 'perhaps'; the doubled in+tanto+in+quanto frames the short interval being compared. 'un quadrel' is an indefinite noun phrase ('a small piece' / 'a little something'); 'posa e vola' are present-tense verbs 'rests and flies' used to render a very short, almost instantaneous action; 'e da la noce si dischiava' completes the image — 'and separated itself from the walnut' with the reflexive 'si' marking the action as taking place to the item itself. The verbs are simple forms used here to sketch a quick sequence (present for vividness).
segments.paradiso-2-g23: 'Giunto mi vidi' is a compact, slightly inverted expression: 'giunto' (past participle of 'giungere') functions adverbially/participially = 'having come/arrived,' and 'mi vidi' (passato remoto 'vidi' with clitic 'mi') means 'I found myself' or 'I saw myself.' The following relative clause 'ove mirabil cosa mi torse il viso a sé' uses 'ove' = 'where' and 'torse' = passato remoto of 'torcere' ('twisted/turned'), so literally 'where a wondrous thing turned my face toward itself.' The sequence of past tenses describes completed events.
segments.paradiso-2-g24: 'e però' links with the previous clause meaning 'and therefore' or 'and so.' 'quella cui non potea mia cura essere ascosa' is a relative clause whose antecedent is the feminine demonstrative 'quella' ('she/that one'); 'cui' = 'to whom/for whom'; 'non potea' is imperfect of 'potere' ('could not'); 'mia cura essere ascosa' literally 'my care be concealed' — the infinitive 'essere' plus past participle 'ascosa' meaning 'hidden.' The sense: 'she, to whom my concern could not be hidden.' Note that 'mia cura' is the thing that cannot be hidden to her.
segments.paradiso-2-g25: 'volta ver’ me' is compact: 'volta' is the feminine past participle of 'volgere' used predicatively and agreeing with 'quella' (she), so 'having turned'; 'ver’' is a poetic contraction of 'verso' meaning 'toward'; and 'me' is the indirect object 'me' — 'she turned toward me.'
segments.paradiso-2-g26: 'sì lieta come bella' is a comparison/intensification: 'sì' here = 'so/so very,' 'lieta' = 'glad/joyful,' and 'come bella' = 'as (she was) beautiful/fair.' The short phrase links emotional state ('lieta') with appearance ('bella').
segments.paradiso-2-g27: This is a direct command addressed by Beatrice. 'Drizza' is the (singular) imperative of 'drizzare' meaning 'direct/raise/turn'; 'la mente' is the direct object 'the mind'; 'in Dio' is the prepositional complement 'to God' or 'toward God'; 'grata' is an adjective agreeing with 'mente' (feminine), best rendered 'grateful' or 'pleasing' — 'Direct your mind, grateful, to God' or 'Direct your grateful mind to God.'
segments.paradiso-2-g28: 'mi disse' is the simple narrative past: 'disse' is passato remoto of 'dire' (3rd person singular) and 'mi' is the indirect object pronoun 'to me' — 'she said to me.'
segments.paradiso-2-g29: The reported clause begins with the relative 'che' = 'that/who.' 'n’ ha' is an archaic/clitic form of 'ne ha' (here functioning as a clitic particle tied to the auxiliary), followed by 'congiunti' (past participle 'joined') with the auxiliary 'ha' — 'has joined (us)'; 'con la prima stella' is a prepositional phrase 'with the first star.' The sense is 'who has joined us to the first star.'
segments.paradiso-2-g30: 'Parev’ a me' is a contracted poetic form of 'pareva a me' (imperfect of 'parere') meaning 'it seemed to me.' The clause 'che nube ne coprisse' follows; after verbs of seeming ('pareva') Dante uses the subjunctive, so 'coprisse' is the imperfect subjunctive of 'coprire' ('that a cloud might cover it'). 'ne' is a clitic meaning 'of it/from it/around it' referring backward to the thing being described. 'lucida' is an adjective ('bright/shining') modifying 'nube.'
segments.paradiso-2-g31: Single adjective 'spessa' continues the series describing 'nube' — 'thick' or 'dense.' It agrees in gender/number with 'nube' and is placed in a descriptive list.
segments.paradiso-2-g32: 'solida e pulita' are two more coordinated adjectives ('solid' and 'clean/clear') modifying the same noun 'nube.' The conjunction 'e' links them; placement after the previous adjectives continues the descriptive catalog.
segments.paradiso-2-g33: 'quasi adamante' introduces a simile 'almost like a diamond.' The relative clause 'che lo sol ferisse' uses 'che' = 'which/that'; 'lo' stands for the cloud ('it'); 'sol' is poetic for 'sole'/'solo' (the sun alone), and 'ferisse' is the imperfect subjunctive of 'ferire' used in the comparative clause — 'which only the sun might strike' or 'as if only the sun struck it.' The subjunctive marks the hypothetical/comparative tone.
segments.paradiso-2-g34: 'Per entro sé' is an emphatic, slightly redundant locative phrase meaning 'into itself' or 'within itself' (per + entro). 'l’etterna margarita' is the feminine subject 'the eternal pearl.' 'ne' is a clitic pronoun referring back to what was covered/struck (the light/image) and 'ricevette' is passato remoto (3rd singular) of 'ricevere' meaning 'received' or 'took in.' The order highlights the inward reception (particle + verb).
segments.paradiso-2-g35: 'com’ acqua recepe' = 'as water receives' ('com’' = 'come'). 'recepe' is an archaic present form of 'ricevere' (3rd singular) used here to give a general simile. 'raggio di luce' = 'a ray of light' (direct object of 'recepe'). 'permanendo' is a gerund ('remaining') and 'unita' is a past participle/adjective agreeing with the pearly/elemental subject ('united' or 'joined'), so 'while remaining united.' The gerund indicates that the reception and the state of unity occur simultaneously.
segments.paradiso-2-g100: Ben che is a concessive phrase meaning "although"; Ben here functions as an intensifier introducing a concessive subordinate clause. Nel quanto tanto is a poetic locution meaning "to such an extent / as far as" (literally "in the how-much so-much") and sets the limit being described. Non si stenda is a present subjunctive with the reflexive si used impersonally/passively ("does not extend / reach"), and la vista più lontana places the noun vista before the qualifying adjective più lontana in typical Italian order.
segments.paradiso-2-g101: Lì vedrai is future indicative (second person singular) meaning "there you will see." Come introduces an explanatory clause. Convien is an impersonal present ("it is fitting / necessary") that governs a subordinate clause introduced by ch’ (contraction of che), so risplenda is in the present subjunctive expressing what must or ought to happen; igualmente is an adverb modifying risplenda. The structure is: you will see how it is fitting that it shine equally.
segments.paradiso-2-g102: Or is an adverb meaning "now/then" that opens the following simile; it functions as a discourse marker shifting the narrative into comparison.
segments.paradiso-2-g103: Come ai colpi de li caldi rai introduces a simile with come ("as/like"). Ai colpi is a dative/locative phrase (a + i) meaning "to/by the blows/strokes." De/li caldi rai is a genitive phrase "of the warm rays." Riman nudo il suggetto is a present indicative construction where riman (remain) takes the predicate nudo ("naked/stripped") and il suggetto ("the subject/thing affected") as its grammatical subject. E dal colore e dal freddo primai links the two consequences "in color and in its former cold" using dal (da + il) meaning "from/in regard to." Primai is an archaic/poetic form meaning "formerly/at first."
segments.paradiso-2-g104: Così links the previous simile to the main point ("thus/so"). Rimaso is a past participle used adjectivally ("remaining/being left") that modifies te ne l’intelletto: te (you) + ne (in/of it) + l’intelletto (the intellect) yields "remaining thus in your intellect." Voglio informar is a periphrastic construction with voglio (present indicative of volere, "I wish/intend") followed by the infinitive informar (archaic infinitive often written without final -e) meaning "to inform/shape/impart." Di luce sì vivace is the complement: di luce = "with/of light," sì vivace = "so/so very vivid," where sì is an intensifier.
segments.paradiso-2-g105: Che introduces a relative/consecutive clause referring back to the light just mentioned. Ti is the indirect object "to you" and tremolerà is future indicative (third person singular) of tremolare, meaning "it will tremble/quake/waver." Nel suo aspetto is a locative phrase (in + il) meaning "in its aspect/appearance," where suo agrees with the light (its). The clause says the vivid light will cause a trembling toward you in the way it appears.
segments.paradiso-2-g106: The whole clause: 'Dentro dal ciel de la divina pace' = 'within/from the heaven of the divine peace' (archaic 'dal' = 'da il'). 'Si gira un corpo' uses the reflexive 'si' with 'girare' to mean 'a body turns/rotates' (present indicative). 'Ne la cui virtute' = 'in whose virtue/power' (relative 'cui'='whose'). 'L’esser di tutto suo contento giace' uses the infinitive with the article 'l' to make 'the being/existence' ('l'esser') the subject, 'di tutto suo contento' = 'of all its content' (possessive 'suo' refers to the body), and 'giace' (present indicative of 'giacere') = 'lies' or 'rests.'
segments.paradiso-2-g107: Lo is the archaic/poetic masculine definite article (il) used before the masculine noun; ciel is a shortened poetic form of cielo meaning the celestial sphere/heaven. Seguente is an adjective meaning “next” or “following” and follows the noun, a normal Italian order here (article + noun + adjective).
segments.paradiso-2-g108: C’ha is a contracted form: the c’ here represents an older/poetic clitic (essentially ‘che’ = ‘which’) followed by ha, the third-person singular present of avere, so together ‘which has’. Tante is the feminine plural adjective agreeing with vedute and means ‘so many’ or ‘many’. Vedute is the plural of veduta, ‘sights’/‘views’ (here plural, concrete perceptions or visions).
segments.paradiso-2-g109: Quell’ is the elided form of quello (the apostrophe marks the dropped vowel) and here acts as a demonstrative ‘that’ or ‘the one’. Esser is the infinitive essere used substantively (‘being’); it is a nominalized verb form. Parte is a noun ‘part’; per introduces the manner or medium here (‘through’/‘by means of’), and diverse essenze is a noun phrase ‘different/various essences’ with the adjective after the noun, as is common in Italian.
segments.paradiso-2-g110: Da expresses source or origin here (‘from’/‘by’), and lui is the tonic third-person pronoun ‘him’ (used after the preposition). Distratte and contenute are past participles (feminine plural) agreeing with the feminine plural noun they modify (essences or vedute); distratte means ‘drawn away/separated’ or ‘distracted’ in a passive/result sense, and contenute means ‘contained’ or ‘held’ (passive sense). The coordinating e links the two parallel participial phrases.
segments.paradiso-2-g72: Ancor is an archaic/poetic adverb equivalent to modern ancora, meaning “still” or “yet.” Placed frontally for emphasis; it does not change the mood or tense of the following clause, only the discourse flow.
segments.paradiso-2-g73: This is a protasis introduced by the conditional subordinate se + imperfect subjunctive fosse (fosse = essere in the imperfect subjunctive) expressing a hypothetical: “if ... were.” Raro here is an adjectival predicate meaning “rare/scarce,” and di quel bruno cagion is a prepositional phrase “of that dark cause” with che tu dimandi as a relative clause “which you ask about.” Tu dimandi is present (Dante uses dimandi for you ask/request).
segments.paradiso-2-g74: This alternative clause begins with o (“or”). D’oltre in parte = “in the outer part / farther out”; fora is an archaic verbal form (here functioning like fosse) meaning “were” / “would be” (imperfect subjunctive/conditional usage in older Italian). Di sua materia = “of its matter”; sì digiuno = “so deprived / so lacking.” Esto is an archaic demonstrative = questo (“this”), and pianeto = planet.
segments.paradiso-2-g75: A single coordinating o = “or,” introducing the next comparison; purely connective.
segments.paradiso-2-g76: Sì come = “just as / in the same way that.” Comparte is 3rd person singular present of compartire / compartare (here “shares / is distributed between”), used to say how fat and lean are disposed in one body. Lo grasso e ’l magro un corpo = “the fat and the lean (in) one body,” a natural-example simile to follow.
segments.paradiso-2-g77: Così = “thus / so.” Questo = “this one” (referring to the planet). Nel suo volume = “in its bulk/volume” (volume used in the sense of mass/bulk). Cangerebbe is the conditional form (–ebbe) of an older verb cangere (cambiare), so “would change / would swap”; carte here means “parts” or “portions” to be exchanged — literally “pages/pieces,” used metaphorically as Dante often does.
segments.paradiso-2-g78: Se + imperfect subjunctive fosse establishes another conditional clause: “If the first were (the case).” ’l is the elided article (il), primo = “first” (referring back to the first hypothesis). Fosse is the imperfect subjunctive of essere used in hypothetical condition.
segments.paradiso-2-g79: Fora manifesto = an older / poetic verbal form fora (parallel to fosse) + adjective manifesto “evident,” meaning “would be evident.” Ne l’eclissi del sol = “in the eclipse of the sun”; ne = in/within, l’ eclissi = the eclipse, del = of the. Note: sol here is Dante’s form for sole (sun).
segments.paradiso-2-g80: Per + infinitive trasparere gives cause/result: “because / by showing through.” Trasparere = to shine through / to become visible through something. Lo lume = “the light.” Come in altro raro ingesto is a simile: come = “as,” in altro = “in some other,” raro = “rare / thin / rarefied,” ingesto is a rare/obscure nominal form here (context: some rarefied medium or attenuation) — read as “attenuation / rarefied medium” that lets the light show through.
segments.paradiso-2-g81: 'Questo' is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this/this one' and is the subject of the clause; 'non è' is the present indicative of 'essere' with negation and means 'is not' (i.e. 'this is not so'). The colon marks a pause leading into the consequence in the next segment.
segments.paradiso-2-g82: Però here functions as 'therefore' (drawing a conclusion from the previous clause). 'È da vedere' is an impersonal/idiomatic construction: 'è' + 'da' + infinitive ('vedere') expresses that something 'must be seen/considered' (necessity or requirement). 'De l’altro' = 'of/from the other' (archaic 'de' = modern 'di' + elided article), indicating the viewpoint or source from which the matter must be considered.
segments.paradiso-2-g83: The clause begins with 'e' ('and'). 'S'elli' is an archaic contraction of 'se' + 'elli' and functions impersonally here ('if it happens/if it be that'); 'avvien' (3rd sing. present of avvenire) = 'it happens.' 'Ch’' (= che) introduces the subordinate clause. 'Io' is the emphatic first-person subject. 'L’altro' is the direct object ('the other'), and 'cassi' is a present subjunctive (poetic/archaic spelling) meaning 'I should reject/annul' (subjunctive after the impersonal 'it happens that...').
segments.paradiso-2-g84: 'Falsificato' is a past participle used predicatively ('falsified'). 'Fia' is a literary/future form of the auxiliary 'essere' used here to mean 'will be' (future passive: 'will be falsified'). 'Lo tuo parere' is archaic word order with the article 'lo' preceding the possessive 'tuo' + noun 'parere' = 'your opinion/judgment.'
segments.paradiso-2-g85: 'S’elli' is an archaic contraction (roughly 'se elli') used impersonally/introducing a conditional ('if it is that...'). 'È' is present indicative 'is' and 'che' introduces the subordinate clause. 'Questo raro' = 'this rare/thin [light]' (demonstrative + adjective). 'Non trapassi' is a negated present subjunctive/optative of 'trapassare' (poetic usage) meaning 'does not pass through' or 'does not penetrate'; the subjunctive is used after the 'è che' conditional formulation.
segments.paradiso-2-g86: 'Esser conviene' is an impersonal expression meaning 'it is fitting/necessary that there be.' 'Un termine' = 'a limit/terminus.' 'Da onde' = 'from which' (archaic word order: 'da' + 'onde'). 'Lo suo contrario' ('its opposite') uses the article 'lo' before the possessive 'suo'—a common older/poetic pattern. 'Più' here means 'any longer/no longer,' and 'passar non lassi' expresses that the opposite 'no longer passes' (the verb 'lassi' is an archaic/poetic form related to allowance/letting and is used here in the sense 'let/allow to pass').
segments.paradiso-2-g87: 'E indi' = 'and thence/from there.' 'L’altrui raggio' = 'the other's/another's ray' (l' + altrui). 'Si rifonde' is a reflexive/passive-like present form meaning 'is poured back' or 'is reflected' ('si' marks the reflexive/passive sense, 'rifonde' = 'sends back/refunds/reflects'). 'Così come' = 'just as/like.' 'Color torna per vetro' = 'color (the color/light) returns through glass.' The relative clause 'lo qual di retro a sé piombo nasconde' literally: 'which on its back hides lead to itself,' i.e. the glass has lead on the reverse that conceals/backs it, so the color is reflected back; note the inverted/poetic order 'di retro a sé' = 'on its back.'
segments.paradiso-2-g60: Ed is the euphonic form of e ("and") used before a vowel; ella is the stressed subject pronoun "she" introducing direct speech. The colon marks that what follows is her spoken words.
segments.paradiso-2-g61: Certo assai is an emphatic adverbial phrase meaning "very certainly"; vedrai is the simple future (2nd person singular) of vedere, here meaning "you will see/you will find/you will realise." Sommerso is a past participle used predicatively ("overwhelmed/submerged"); nel falso is a prepositional phrase "in falsehood" and il creder tuo is the noun phrase "your belief" serving as the thing that will be seen overwhelmed — the object appears after the predicate in this poetic word order.
segments.paradiso-2-g62: se bene ascolti is a protasis using the present indicative "if you listen well/heed." L'argomentar is the infinitive used as a noun "the arguing/argument(s)." ch'io introduces a subordinate clause with the explicit subject io; li is an object clitic (plural) referring back to what her arguments will oppose, and farò is the future of fare ("I will make/bring"); avverso functions adverbially/locatively here meaning "against" or "in opposition to." The clitic li appears before the finite verb farò (standard clitic placement).
segments.paradiso-2-g63: La spera ottava = "the eighth sphere" (spera is an archaic/poetic form for sfera). Vi is the dative/locative "to you" placed before the verb; dimostra is present indicative "shows" and molti lumi is the direct object "many lights." The adjective ottava follows the noun, a normal pattern in medieval/poetic word order.
segments.paradiso-2-g64: li quali is the plural relative "which" referring to the lights. The repeated e...e links parallel phrases: nel quale = "in which [place/form]" and nel quanto = "in how much/degree." Notar si posson is a poetic ordering of the impersonal/passive construction (notar + si + posson) meaning "can be noticed/observed," with si marking the passive/impersonal and posson (possono) the ability. di diversi volti means "by different faces/appearances," i.e. by different aspects or looks.
segments.paradiso-2-g65: Se introduces a conditional clause "if." Raro e denso are adjectival qualities ("rare and dense"); ciò is the demonstrative "that/this." Facesser is an older third-person plural subjunctive (or potential) of fare, used here to express the hypothetical "would/do make" or "produced"; tanto modifies the extent "so much/to such an extent." The ordering is poetic, with the subject qualities placed before the verb.
segments.paradiso-2-g66: una sola virtù is the noun phrase "one single virtue." Sarebbe is the conditional of essere ("would be"); in tutti means "in all" (i.e. present in everyone or in all things). The phrase states the hypothetical result: that one virtue would be found universally.
segments.paradiso-2-g67: più e men = "more or less" (literally "more and less"); distributa is a past participle agreeing with virtù ("distributed"); e altrettanto (= altrettanto) means "and just as much/likewise," i.e. indicating that the distribution would be roughly the same across individuals. The short clause expresses that distribution would only differ in degree.
segments.paradiso-2-g68: Virtù diverse is the subject "different virtues." Esser convegnon is an older/poetic construction: convegnon (from convegnere) "it is fitting/it accords" combined with the infinitive esser yields the sense "ought to be / must be." Frutti di princìpi formali is the predicate "fruits of formal principles," i.e. results that follow from formal causes. The whole line asserts that different virtues arise from different formal principles.
segments.paradiso-2-g69: e quei is the resumptive demonstrative phrase "and those," picking up the antecedent (the principles) to introduce the following qualification; the comma signals a parenthetical turn.
segments.paradiso-2-g70: for ch'uno is an archaic concessive/exceptive phrase: for (here "but/except") + ch' (che) + uno = "except for one" or "were it not for one." It sets up that one principle would be an exception.
segments.paradiso-2-g71: seguiterieno is the conditional form (they would follow) of seguire in an older/metrical spelling; a tua ragion means "according to your reasoning/argument." Distrutti is a past participle meaning "destroyed"; together the clause says that, by your reasoning, those principles (but for one) would be destroyed — i.e. shown not to stand.
segments.paradiso-2-g88: Or = now. dirai is the 2nd-person singular future of dire (‘you will say’), with the subject tu stated for emphasis. ch’el = che + el, a subordinate conjunction + archaic subject pronoun (‘that it’/’that he’) introducing the clause; si dimostra is the pronominal verb dimostrarsi in the present (shows itself / appears), tetro is the predicate adjective (gloomy). The word order ivi lo raggio (there the ray) is inverted for emphasis, and più che in altre parti is a comparative phrase ‘more than in other parts/places.’
segments.paradiso-2-g89: per + infinitive clause here gives the causal sense ‘because/for’ (per esser … = because it is …). esser is the infinitive of essere ‘to be’; lì = there; refratto is a past participle used adjectivally (‘refracted’); più a retro = a comparative phrase ‘more to the rear / further back.’
segments.paradiso-2-g90: Da questa instanza = ‘from this instance/example’ (da = from). The word order places esperïenza (experience) after the verb: può deliberarti is ‘can (può) deliberate/decide for you / make you conclude’ with the clitic -ti attached. Here esperïenza is the logical subject (‘experience can tell/decide for you’).
segments.paradiso-2-g91: se già mai la provi = conditional clause ‘if ever you try it.’ se = if; già mai is a common combination meaning ‘ever’/‘at any time’; la is the feminine direct object clitic referring back to the matter just mentioned; provi is 2nd-person singular present of provare (‘you try/you test/you experience’) used in the protasis.
segments.paradiso-2-g92: ch’ = che ‘that’ introducing the content clause. esser suol = the infinitive esser with suol (verb ‘is wont’) yields ‘is wont to be / usually is’; fonte = source/spring; ai rivi = a + i ‘to the’/‘at the’ streams (rivi = small rivers/streams); di vostr’ arti = ‘of your arts/skills’ (vostr’ = your, arti = arts/skills). The whole phrase means ‘that it is usually the source of the streams of your arts.’
segments.paradiso-2-g93: Tre specchi prenderai: Tre = three, specchi = mirrors (plural noun), prenderai = future 2nd-person singular ‘you will take.’ The short clause is an instruction framed as a future tense.
segments.paradiso-2-g94: e i due rimovi da te d’un modo = ‘and you remove the two (of them) from you in one way.’ rimovi is a 2nd-person form of rimovere used instructively (present/imperative sense ‘remove’), i due = the two, da te = away from you, d’un modo = ‘in one manner/way.’
segments.paradiso-2-g95: e l’altro = ‘and the other (one).’ Simple coordination introducing the third mirror treated differently.
segments.paradiso-2-g96: più rimosso = comparative phrase ‘more removed / farther away,’ modifying l’altro (the other mirror is placed at a greater distance).
segments.paradiso-2-g97: tr’ambo li primi li occhi tuoi ritrovi = ‘between the two the first (nearest) your eyes you will find again.’ tr’ambo (tra ambo) = among/between the two; li primi = the first/two foremost (the two previously indicated); li occhi tuoi = your eyes as direct object; ritrovi (2nd-person present) = ‘you find again / you will find’ (present used with future sense after the initial future/instruction).
segments.paradiso-2-g98: Rivolto ad essi = Rivolto is a past participle used adjectivally/passive (‘turned toward’ / ‘facing’); ad essi = toward them (ess i = third-person pronoun). The phrase sets the orientation: face the mirrors.
segments.paradiso-2-g99: fa che … = fa (imperative/command ‘make/do’) introduces a subordinate clause (che + subjunctives). dopo il dosso = ‘behind the hump/your back’; ti stea un lume = stea is an archaic subjunctive of stare (‘let there be / let it stand’), with clitic ti indicating the light is placed with respect to you (‘be to you’ / ‘behind you there shall stand a light’); che i tre specchi accenda = che + accenda (subjunctive of accendere) ‘which lights/illumines the three mirrors’; e torni a te da tutti ripercosso = and (may it) return to you, da tutti ripercosso = ‘reflected/reverberated by all (of them).’
segments.paradiso-2-g111: Li altri is the subject ('the others'); giron is an archaic 3rd-person-plural verb form meaning 'they turn/circle' (older -on ending instead of modern -ano). Per varie differenze is a prepositional phrase 'through/by means of various distinctions' modifying how they circle. Le distinzion ... hanno is a noun phrase plus a relative clause: le distinzion is the subject of dispongono, che introduces the relative clause 'that they have within themselves' (dentro da sé hanno). Dispongono is 3rd-person plural present indicative 'they arrange/dispose'; a lor fini e lor semenze is the prepositional complement 'toward their ends and their seeds' (lor = their, plural possessive before the noun).
segments.paradiso-2-g112: Questi organi del mondo is the subject group ('these organs/instruments of the world'); così vanno is the predicate in present indicative 3rd-person plural ('thus go/move'). The phrase treats 'organi' metaphorically as the means or instruments by which the world functions.
segments.paradiso-2-g113: come tu vedi omai is a parenthetical clause 'as you see now' (come = as; tu vedi = you see, present indicative; omai = by now/now). It comments on the previous statement and addresses the addressee directly.
segments.paradiso-2-g114: di grado in grado is an idiom meaning 'from degree to degree' or 'step by step'; di introduces the measure ('of degree'), repeated for emphasis in an intransitive distributive expression.
segments.paradiso-2-g115: che introduces a relative clause referring back to the organs/giri: 'which take from above and make below.' Di sù prendono = 'they take from above' (prendono 3rd-pl present). E di sotto fanno = 'and they make/produce below' (fanno 3rd-pl). The subject is the same plural entity introduced earlier (the organs/turns).
segments.paradiso-2-g116: Riguarda bene omai is an imperative/instructive phrase: 'look carefully now' or 'consider well now' (riguarda as an imperative or present imperative sense). Sì com' io vado is a comparative clause 'how I go' or 'in the way I go' (com = come/as, io vado = I go). Per questo loco al vero che disiri gives purpose/direction: 'through this place toward the truth that you desire' (al = a + il 'to the', vero = 'truth', che disiri = relative clause 'that you desire').
segments.paradiso-2-g117: sì che = 'so that'; poi = 'afterwards'; sappi here is an optative/subjunctive sense addressed to 'you' ('may you know' or 'you might learn'), an archaic or dialectal form (modern Italian would use sappia). Sol = 'only' or 'merely'; tener lo guado = 'to hold/keep the ford' where tener is the infinitive 'to hold' and lo guado = 'the ford' (a crossing point).
segments.paradiso-2-g118: Lo moto e la virtù is a compound subject 'the motion and the virtue/power'; d’i santi giri is a genitive phrase 'of the holy turns/circuits' (d’i = di + i). The whole line names the active principles (motion and power) belonging to the sacred rotations.
segments.paradiso-2-g119: come introduces a simile 'as/like'; dal fabbro l'arte del martello is the image 'from the smith, the art/skill of the hammer' (dal = from the; l'arte = the art/skill; del martello = of the hammer). The clause compares how motion/virtue relate to their mover with how a smith's skill is realized in his hammer.
segments.paradiso-2-g120: da' beati motor convien che spiri is an impersonal construction: 'it is fitting/necessary that (they) breathe from the blessed Mover.' Convien is an impersonal verb 'it is necessary/it behoves'; che introduces a subjunctive clause (spiri = 3rd-sing. subjunctive of spirare 'breathe'/'be breathed'); da' (da i) beati motor = 'from the blessed Mover' is the source.
segments.paradiso-2-g121: e 'l ciel cui tanti lumi fanno bello links the next thought: 'and the heaven, to/for which so many lights make it beautiful' (cui = which/to which; tanti lumi = so many lights; fanno bello = make (it) beautiful). The relative clause describes the heaven by the multitude of lights that adorn it.
segments.paradiso-2-g122: de la mente profonda che lui volve is a genitive/relative phrase 'from the profound/deep mind that turns it' (de = of/from; mente profonda = deep mind; che lui volve = that turns it — here lui functions as a neuter pronoun referring back to the heaven). Prende l’image = 'it takes the image' (prende = takes); e fassene suggello is an archaic clustered form meaning 'and makes of it a seal' (fassene = fa + se/ne, i.e. 'makes of it' or 'forms from it' and suggello = 'a seal/mark').
segments.paradiso-2-g123: E come introduces a simile ('And as'). l’alma is the elided article + noun ('the soul'). dentro a vostra polve uses dentro + a to mean 'within (your) dust'; vostra is a possessive agreeing with polve. per differenti membra e conformate a diverse potenze is a prepositional phrase 'through different members and formed (conformate, a past participle agreeing with membra) to diverse powers' that expresses the means and the resulting arrangement. si risolve is the present reflexive verb risolversi (3rd singular), meaning 'is resolved/dissolved' — the clitic si marks the reflexive/passive sense and attaches to the finite verb.
segments.paradiso-2-g124: così links back as the result ('so'/'thus'). l’intelligenza is the elided article + noun 'the intellect/understanding'. sua bontate places the possessive sua before bontate (goodness), indicating 'its goodness' or 'goodness proper to it.' multiplicata per le stelle is a past participle phrase ('multiplied by the stars') modifying bontate (multiplicata agrees in gender/number with the noun it qualifies). spiega is a present indicative (3rd singular) of spiegare used here in the sense 'unfolds/displays' — the main verb of the result clause.
segments.paradiso-2-g125: girando is a gerund ('turning'/'revolving') that gives the manner or accompaniment of the action just stated. Sé is the reflexive pronoun used with girare ('itself'), and here it refers back to the intelligence (or its goodness) as the thing turning. sovra is a preposition meaning 'above'/'over' and sua unitate is a possessive + noun ('its unity'); unitate (archaic spelling) = unità. The sequence expresses: while unfolding, it turns itself above its unity.
segments.paradiso-2-g126: Virtù diversa is the subject ('a different/particular virtue'); fa is present indicative (3rd singular) of fare used transitively here meaning 'makes' or 'forms'. diversa lega is the object ('a different tie/bond'—lega as 'bond/league/connection'). col is the contraction of con + il ('with the') and prezïoso (diaeresis marks vowel break in Dantean spelling) modifies corpo ('the precious body'). ch’ella avviva is a relative clause: ch' is che (elided), ella refers back to virtù, and avviva is 3rd singular present (avvivo) meaning 'enlivens' or 'gives life to' — form matches either indicative or subjunctive shape in this position, but here reads as a plain relative with present sense.
segments.paradiso-2-g127: nel qual is a short relative phrase: nel (in + il) = 'in the' and qual is the relative 'which' (poetic/archaic form of quale). The phrase introduces the clause specifying 'in which'.
segments.paradiso-2-g128: sì come is a comparative 'just as' or 'in the same way as.' vita in voi is a simple noun phrase 'life in you' (voi = you, plural or respectful). The whole phrase establishes the parallel: 'in which, just as life is in you...'.
segments.paradiso-2-g129: si lega is a reflexive construction of legare (3rd singular present): si marks the reflexive/passive sense, so it can mean 'it binds itself' or 'it is bound' depending on context; the subject is the previously mentioned virtue (ella). The verb finishes the clause introduced by 'nel qual'.
segments.paradiso-2-g130: Per here is causal/agentive 'by' or 'because of' ('through/on account of'). la natura lieta is the noun + adjective 'the glad/favourable nature' (lieta follows and agrees). onde is a relative/adverbial 'from which' or 'whence', and deriva is 3rd singular present of derivare 'derives/originates.' The whole phrase gives the source/cause: 'because of the glad nature from which it originates.'
segments.paradiso-2-g131: la virtù mista is the subject 'the mixed/mingled virtue' (mista = past participle used adjectivally). per lo corpo uses per with the definite article to mean 'through the body' or 'in the body.' luce here functions as a poetic present verb (from Latin lucere) — 'shines' or 'gives light.' come introduces a simile 'like/as', letizia is the compared noun 'joy', per pupilla viva uses per in the sense 'to/for' and pupilla viva = 'a living pupil (of the eye)'. The comparison says the virtue shining in the body is like joy shining in a living pupil.
segments.paradiso-2-g132: Da essa ('from it') uses da to mark origin. vien is an archaic present form of venire used impersonally/actively ('comes'). ciò che = 'that which.' da luce a luce is a repetitive prepositional phrase 'from light to light' meaning 'from one degree of light to another' (da ... a ...). par is an older form of pare/pare (3rd singular) meaning 'seems' or 'appears,' and differente is the predicate adjective 'different.' Together: 'From it comes that which appears different from one light to another.'
segments.paradiso-2-g133: non da negates the following origin clause ('not from'). denso e raro are adjectives meaning 'dense and rare' (here 'dense and thin/sparse') and function as the opposed material causes Dante denies: the difference is not due to density or rarity.
segments.paradiso-2-g134: essa è establishes the identity ('it is'). formal principio is a noun phrase 'formal principle' (formal = pertaining to form). che produce is a relative clause with che = 'that' and produce = 3rd singular present of produrre, 'produces' or 'brings forth.'
segments.paradiso-2-g135: conforme a sua bontà is a prepositional phrase 'according to/in conformity with its goodness' (conforme a = 'in accordance with'; sua bontà = 'its goodness'). The phrase qualifies how the formal principle produces the effects.
segments.paradiso-2-g136: lo turbo e ’l chiaro lists the two effects: lo and ’l are definite articles (lo used before certain consonant clusters, ’l is elided il). turbo is a noun 'turmoil/whirlwind/storm' and chiaro is used as noun 'brightness/clarity.' The construction names 'the turmoil and the brightness' produced by the formal principle.

sentences.paradiso-2-s14: She smiled a little, and then, "If the opinion errs," she said to me, "concerning mortals where the key of sense does not unlock, certainly the shafts of astonishment should no longer sting you; for, after the senses, you see that reason has short wings."
sentences.paradiso-2-s15: But tell me what you yourself think of it.
sentences.paradiso-2-s16: And I: "What appears different to us up here, I believe, is caused by bodies that are rare and dense."
sentences.paradiso-2-s9: If I were a body, and here one cannot conceive how another dimension could endure, that it must be, if body returns into body, the desire to see that essence in which one sees how our nature and God were united would no longer need to be kindled in me.
sentences.paradiso-2-s10: There will be seen there what we hold by faith, not demonstrated, but it will be known in itself in the manner of the first truth that man believes.
sentences.paradiso-2-s11: I answered: 'My lady, as devout as I can be, I thank him who has taken me away from the mortal world.'
sentences.paradiso-2-s12: But tell me: what are the dark marks of this body, which down there on earth make others invent stories about Cain?
sentences.paradiso-2-s1: O you who are in a little boat, eager to hear and following behind my little bark which, singing, plows onward, turn back to look again at your shores; do not put out onto the open sea, for perhaps, if you lose me, you would be left bewildered.
sentences.paradiso-2-s2: The water that I draw has never before been plied; Minerva breathes, and Apollo leads me, and the nine Muses show me the Bears.
sentences.paradiso-2-s3: You few who early raised your neck to the bread of the angels (by which one lives here but does not go away sated), you may well set your vessel upon the high sea, keeping my wake before the water that returns unchanged.
sentences.paradiso-2-s4: Those glorious ones who went to Colchis did not marvel as you will do, when Jason saw himself made a plowman.
sentences.paradiso-2-s5: The hardened and perpetual thirst of that misshapen realm bore us away from there swiftly, almost as you see the heavens.
sentences.paradiso-2-s6: Beatrice above, and I looked at her; and perhaps in as brief an instant as a little piece rests and flies and detaches itself from the walnut, I found myself arrived where a wondrous thing turned my face to itself; and so she, to whom my concern could not be hidden, having turned toward me, as joyful as she was fair, said to me, 'Direct your mind, grateful, to God,' 'who has joined us to the first star.'
sentences.paradiso-2-s7: It seemed to me that a cloud covered it — bright, thick, solid and clear — almost like a diamond that the sun alone would strike.
sentences.paradiso-2-s8: Within itself the eternal pearl received it, as water receives a ray of light while remaining united.
sentences.paradiso-2-s29: Although the sight does not reach so far, there you will see how it must shine equally.
sentences.paradiso-2-s30: Now, just as by the strokes of the warm rays the snow's subject remains stripped both in color and in its former cold, so, remaining thus in your intellect, I wish to inform you with such a vivid light that it will tremble toward you in its aspect.
sentences.paradiso-2-s31: Within the heaven of divine peace a body turns; in whose virtue the being of all its content lies.
sentences.paradiso-2-s32: The next heaven, which has so many sights, that being a part, through different essences, drawn from him and contained by him.
sentences.paradiso-2-s21: Still: if that dark cause you ask about were scarce, or if this planet were in its outer part so empty of its own matter, or—just as fat and lean share one body—so this one, in its bulk, would exchange its parts.
sentences.paradiso-2-s22: If the first were so, it would be made manifest in the eclipse of the sun, the light showing through as in some other rare attenuation.
sentences.paradiso-2-s23: This is not so: therefore it must be looked at from the other; and if it should happen that I reject the other, your judgment will be falsified.
sentences.paradiso-2-s24: If this rare (thin) light does not pass through, there must be a boundary from which its opposite can no longer pass; and thence the other's ray is sent back, just as color is returned through glass which, on its reverse, hides lead to itself.
sentences.paradiso-2-s17: And she: "You will certainly see your belief overwhelmed by falsehood, if you listen carefully to the argument(s) that I shall bring against it."
sentences.paradiso-2-s18: The eighth sphere displays to you many lights, which, in place and in degree, can be perceived by different appearances.
sentences.paradiso-2-s19: If rarity and density did that to such an extent, a single virtue would be present in all, distributed more or less equally.
sentences.paradiso-2-s20: Different virtues must be the fruits of formal principles, and those principles — except for one — would, following your reasoning, be destroyed.
sentences.paradiso-2-s25: Now you will say that the ray appears gloomier there than in other places, because there it is refracted further back.
sentences.paradiso-2-s26: From this example experience can decide/teach you, if you ever try it, that it is usually the source of the streams of your arts.
sentences.paradiso-2-s27: Take three mirrors; and move two of them away from you in one way, and the other one farther off; between the two first you will find your eyes again.
sentences.paradiso-2-s28: Facing them, arrange that after your back there be a light which lights the three mirrors and comes back to you reflected from all of them.
sentences.paradiso-2-s33: The others circle in diverse distinctions; the distinctions that they have within themselves they arrange for their ends and their seeds.
sentences.paradiso-2-s34: These organs of the world thus move, as you now see, from degree to degree, taking from above and making below.
sentences.paradiso-2-s35: Observe well now how I go through this place toward the truth that you desire, so that afterwards you may only know how to hold the ford.
sentences.paradiso-2-s36: The motion and the virtue of the holy circuits, as the smith's skill is embodied in the hammer, must be breathed from the blessed Mover; and the heaven, whose so many lights make it beautiful, takes the image from the profound mind that turns it and makes it a seal.
sentences.paradiso-2-s37: And as the soul, within your dust, is resolved into diverse powers through different and suitably formed members, so its intelligence, multiplied by its goodness through the stars, unfolds, turning itself above its unity.
sentences.paradiso-2-s38: A distinct virtue makes a distinct bond with the precious body which it enlivens; in that body, just as life is in you, it is bound.
sentences.paradiso-2-s39: Because of the glad nature from which it derives, the virtue mingled with the body shines like joy to a living pupil.
sentences.paradiso-2-s40: From it comes that which appears different from one light to another, not from density and rarity; it is the formal principle that produces, in accordance with its goodness, the turmoil and the brightness.
